sentences.text: The less serious.
Year-- this was about beginning, probably.
Not before.
Not before.
Absolute no.
Because people were coming already from Germany They were coming in Poland.
I think they start coming in '38, '39.
Yes.
And my mother was reading Jewish papers.
And Friday was the day when she was reading.
Everybody was out after dinner.
And she was reading the papers.
She said, come on, Sonia.
I will tell you something.
Take a look what there is in Germany, what they're writing.
I said, Mommy, you know that if they will torture a Jew, the whole city will cry.
That was my answer.
If you touch one Jew--
So the whole city-- no matter in Poland or whatever in Europe, the whole city will cry that they're killing Jews.
That's what I said.
That's what I said, that the Jewish people are crying, all this.
They're crying always.
The crying babies.
I didn't understand something else.
They prepared us-- in the school they were talking to us like that.
They were telling us.
You see?
The teachers in our schools.
They were talking about the Jewish people sometimes, saying that Poland this-- they said that in '17, they were fighting for Poland, the Polish.
And take a look, we find the book.
She said, in the history that they Jewish population said that they were fighting together with Poland for the Polish land.
And this is not true, the Polish teachers were saying.
You see, when you're taking a child-- I was not a child already, even.
I went into school, I probably was about 10, 12 years, that's already-- I was listening very well.
And they were saying about the Jews, always saying that they did it.
This was not true that the Polish people did it by themselves.
They were fighting for the land.
I believed them.
Absolutely I believed them.
My parents said to me like that, you have to listen, but you have to have brains by yourself.
But remember one thing, your brothers are not here-- because my two brothers-- one brother was all this, but is now Israel.
He was studying already in Wilno.
My other brother, Itzchak, was studying in Warsaw, in [INAUDIBLE].
This was a seminar for Jewish teachers, for Hebrew teachers.
Now, I had only two brothers.
Zalman was a year younger than me, but he was in a private gymnasium.
And my little brother, Yankele finished [INAUDIBLE].
There was [NON-ENGLISH], and they hated the Polish.
I said, what are doing to you?
You cannot see that they want to kill us?
I said, no.
I have friends, Polish boys, Polish girls.
They were coming to us.
We are-- went out for dancing together.
They're saying to me, Sonia, wake up.
Wake up.
And my oldest brother, when he went to Israel, my father gave to him a lot of money.
He went with the Aliyah Bet.
You could not get.
So he had a lot of money with him.
Aliyah Bet is words for people that they wanted to go to Israel.
They believed in Israel, in Palestine-- that was in Palestine.
No.
It was not legal, but you have to pay a lot of money.
And my mother and father took my brother to do all this, to Warsaw.
They paid off their money, and they gave it to him.
They were Russian moneta.
This was Russian money in gold.
Yes.
Yes.
We met them.
They lived in our houses.
Not by ours, but in other houses where they need the money.
They took them in.
By us, they were coming to eat.
We were dancing with them.
I had the two friends of mine that they fall in love with them.
One is here.
We were learning about how  they live in the big city, in Warsaw-- how they live, enjoy.
They were college boys.
That was not like us.
They were college boys already.
The German refugees, they were telling us horror stories, terrible stories.
And we couldn't believe this right away.
And they said, yes, you have to believe it.
You have to open your eyes.
We went out with our life in Poland, from Warsaw, because they wanted to kill us.
And then, I didn't believe it that this can be something like that?
Huh?
From Warsaw, yes.
They came from Warsaw.
From Warsaw.
They lived in Warsaw.
No, no.
They came original-- they were Polish, and they lived in Warsaw.
But when the Germans begin to come nearer, they were going away from Poland because they knew.
But people from Poland that lived near the border, they were telling them terrible stories.
In Warsaw.
Right.
Yeah.
Yeah So they were telling us terrible stories.
And I--
No, no.
No, no.
First of all, they came the Russians-- came to us.
The Russians came in the end of '39.
We were very happy that the Polish went away and the Russian came in.
And they were singing with us, and playing with us.
This was in the summer, I think, in July, probably.
July and August they came in.
Because it was hot.
And they were playing outside.
And they were dancing outside.
And they went around with the Jewish girls.
And this was-- they had a beautiful, beautiful time.
I was very much sheltered because I was one daughter in the house.
A little changed, yes.
A little changed.
My mother said, OK, you can go out with your friends, with your friends.
But remember you have to be nine o'clock, eight o'clock home.
Yes.
Because they took away all the food from my father's work for all his life.
They took away everything.
They took away the cheeses.
They took away the meat.
They took away everything.
But my father was such a good man.
He had so many workers in these villages.
They was bringing him-- they were bringing him butter.
They were bringing him flour.
They were bringing us everything.
We were not hungry.
I think it was nice.
It was nice.
They didn't torch us.
They stayed from '39, in summer, till the Germans came in '41.
The Germans came in too probably in August.
In August.
Yes. '39, '40, '41-- two years, they stayed, the Russians.
And then, we begin a little to study Russian.
'39 Till '41, in the middle of '41.
And the end the '41 was already the first killing of the Jews.
When the Germans came to Novogrudok?
Oh, sure.
I remember.
Yes.
They were gentlemen.
When the Germans came to us in Novogrudok, they were gentlemen.
They were young, and they were not SS, SD.
They were young soldiers.
They wanted to flirt.
They wanted to talk to us.
They wanted to dance with us.
They gave us chocolate.
They came into the house like they were our friends.
And you know what they said to my parents?
You have a beautiful daughter.
Make papers for her that she's Irish and send her away because when the SD will come, everything will be kaput.
But my parents, they're not afraid.
I even didn't mention that's what it was telling-- my mother said, don't listen what they're telling you because when we were by the Germans in the first war, they were very good to us.
But the Germans was not the same.
Yes.
Yes.
But I didn't want it.
I could make it because my father was very friendly with the clergyman.
They could make me-- one, two three.
I didn't understand how can I leave my father and mother and my two brothers and go away in someplace that I don't know.
Huh?
Yes.
Yeah, because my mother said, you don't have to be afraid of nothing.
You went in Poland every year for [NON-ENGLISH].
You went to Poland, to Gdynia, to [INAUDIBLE], to Slonsk, to Krakow, all over with the gymnasium, with the schools.
For six weeks, we went around.
We went to Zakopane.
All over, we went.
I never-- in the summer, I went with my mother to Druskienniki.
This is on the border from the Litva and Poland-- only for six weeks.
And then we went to [INAUDIBLE], where the woman was with my brothers there.
Yes, resources, sure.
Resources.
Right away.
In beginning of December, when they begin to talk about this to the Judenrat.
They made the Judenrat.
The Judenrat-- in November '41.
And they begin to ask them-- they need workers to send away to help the army.
And they took the young people, and they send them away.
They never came back.
You know about it.
Now, they took ones, they were [? aggravate. ?] This was something, I don't know.
And they took 50 from the best people of Novogrudok.
They was lawyers, doctors, and they shot them blank [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] in the marketplace in Novogrudok.
Then we knew what is the war.
What is the war, meaning from the war.
After that-- this was in November.
In December, they said that the ghetto has to be built.
Everybody from their houses has to go out and we will build you a ghetto.
You will be living in the ghetto, the Germans.
This was December.
We slept already-- my friends was in my room still.
I slept with my papa and my mama in her bed.
And about two, three o'clock, I was dreaming.
And I wake up with crying, terrible.
Mommy, I got shot in the head from a German.
I waked up my father and mother, and my mother said, OK.
We show them this is a [NON-ENGLISH].
You know what is it?
This is a dream that you have to go away.
Something will be.
Because she never dreamed, Sonia.
And this was the day where they took all of the Jewish people to the graves.
So my mother was very good to my father.
We had a friend, [?
Marvin ?] and he had a brother in Warsaw.
He came with the brother from Warsaw with a small girl-- six, seven years old.
And he didn't know how to take care of her.
So my mother took her to the family.
Yeah?
And when they decided that we have to go away this day.
We have to go away from Novogrudok.
So my mother said, you take the children and go away.
You know where to go.
And I will take the baby, and I will put him in a very good hand.
So we had two horses.
We had whatever.
We had their silver and everything.
And she gave to a neighbor of ours, very rich Polish people.
And she said like that, you go into the house in an hour and take everything that you will find in the house.
Everything is yours.
But remember, you have to watch the baby.
The baby is a six-years-old girl.
She was a beauty.
But remember-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] remember, if you will lose the girl, you will lose your life.
And she left the girl, and she ran away.
She couldn't find already my father and me and my brother because my father saw already the Germans around the house.
So he took us, and  he ran away to the clergyman where he was born, in the same village as my mother was born.
And we were there by the clergyman for six days where the potatoes-- in the [NON-ENGLISH] where the potatoes are kept.
It was very cold, very cold.
But he was very good to us.
He was bringing us this hot water and soup and everything.
But what he said, Mr.
Boldo, you have to go away because the dogs are not letting nobody to sleep.
They're watching what is happening here in the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] neighborhood.
So at night, he took me and my father and my brother.
And he took us about five kilometers.
Then, five kilometers was a lot, a lot.
You're not driving a car.
You're driving your number 11.
And the snow was still here.
And he took us.
And he put in to another neighbor-- for my mother's-- that she went to school with him, the clergyman.
And we stayed there till after the pogrom.
When the ghetto born, he brought us to the ghetto.
I don't remember.
I don't remember.
But he was a fine, fine man.
My father was giving them always-- he was coming to ask-- we had milk in the farm.
He was coming and complaining that he has so many people that they need flour, they need something, this and that.
So my father said, take your horse and come and take whatever you need it.
11 is number 11-- our legs, they were calling 11.
How are you going?
They were saying, with number 11. 11 are the left and the right leg.
There were no cars, no horses.
You couldn't go off with the horses.
They were looking for you.
This was a small village, maybe had five or six houses.
But one house from the other one was far, far, not like one house near the other one-- very near.
We stayed in the house.
We stayed in the-- where the potatoes.
This is up, what they were hearing in the village, what is with the Germans.
You understand?
They were very much afraid of the Germans, too, because the Germans said, if you will keep a Jew, the whole family is going.
The whole family will get killed.
But they were very nice.
They knew my mother.
They knew my father.
And they wanted us to stay with them.
After that, they went to the city.
And they came from the city.
They said, Mr.
Boldo, the ghetto is already ready.
I will take you tomorrow to the ghetto.
And that's what happened.
He took me and my father, my brother to the ghetto.
And the ghetto was full with people.
There were no place for us.
So Lea's mother and father-- Lea, that you had the interview with her.
They had a house, and there were the Judenrat.
Lea [INAUDIBLE] Frydberg.
Her mother her father, they took us in.
And they gave us a small place where we were sleeping on the floor.
They gave us a small piece.
We could already sleep there.
They took it out, all the Jewish people from the houses from the whole city.
And the put them in a small place they called ghetto.
Oh, they made a fence.
Sure, they made a fence with this.
And the police, Polish police, and Ukrainian police, and German police were surrounding us.
The fence, a big fence with the [NON-ENGLISH], iron.
You couldn't go out.
No.
You couldn't go in.
You couldn't go out.
Yes.
Wire, sure.
You couldn't go out.
And they had big dogs.
Big dogs.
Who was guarding?
The ghetto?
Oh, I said to you.
The Polish police, the Ukrainian police, and the Germans.
That's it.
The worst of all of them, they were the Ukrainian.
The Ukrainian were the-- worse from Hitler's.
I didn't count them, but this was-- you know what?
Like you see the bees-- bees with the making honey.
So the whole house was a very small house.
But in one room, maybe it was about 20 people. 20 people, for sure.
And one lived with the other quietly.
And in the morning, they went to work, everybody.
They went out from the ghetto.
They took them to the city because the city was ruined by the bombs.
So they clean up the city.
And they were working there.
And that's it.
No.
No.
How many?
My brother was in the police.
Yankele was in the police, and he was inside the ghetto.
And I was working in the hospital from the ghetto.
They put me in there.
I should not go out to the Germans, and they should hit me over the head because they were-- the people, when they went out to the city, they were killing them.
They were hitting them-- they should work faster, faster, faster.
So my father, he knew to whom to give whatever he gave.
And they put me in the hospital.
And I have the people that they were working with me-- I have here Sonia Orzman.
You saw them on the Novogrudok.
These are my friends from school.
We finished together.
We made the exams to Rostov for dentistry.
Only me and her got accepted to Rostov.
We made the exams.
But the war came in two days.
Everything was finished.
So that's not what you can say something.
And this is not true of this, is it?
Were your parents with you?
Yes.
Always with me.
Always with me.
My father did many things for the Jewish people in the ghetto.
He was very friendly with the head of the police, the Polish police.
He was, all life, he had a family from four children.
And he didn't have-- never enough, so my father was always giving him food for the family.
He gave to him shoes for the family, gave it to him four coats for the family.
Everything.
But when I needed him, he did everything for me, for my father.
My father took it out, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a wife and two children an hour before they took him to the [INAUDIBLE] to kill them.
Because she said that she is something-- a family with us.
So my father went.
He risked his life, and he went to the police.
And he said, the police was [INAUDIBLE], the head of the police of Novogrudok.
He said, [INAUDIBLE], I will do everything for you.
He said, you have to take out the lady with the two daughters.
And he took her out before an hour to finish the whole ghetto.
Now, my husband's two brothers were in there, in the police for dead.
We didn't know because I didn't know my brother.
I didn't know my husband.
I didn't know the brothers.
But his mother, she was coming to the ghetto, asks if somebody can do something for them.
So there were some people, they were asking her $100 in gold.
My husband was telling me about this later.
And they didn't have the gold.
So they went where all the Jews went.
In this time, my father took it out, the woman and the two children.
And they came to the United States.
And they're writing now a book.
[INAUDIBLE] writing a book about this.
You see, a Jew is a big [NON-ENGLISH], a Jew.
He has always a watch, a ring, a pair of shoes, a pair of boots.
And they were changing.
When they were on the market and working, the paisanos, they were coming and said, I need a watch.
I will give you the food.
So they gave to him the watch, the paisano, and he will bring them bread.
Yeah, the peasants.
That's all.
That's the only way.
They couldn't buy it.
There were [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] no food in places to buy.
They were bringing them for the morning, for a ring, for boots, for everything.
And that's what.
And this was a hunger, too, in the ghetto.
A lot of hunger.
But they were given a piece of bread a day because people, they went to work.
So the Germans was giving them a piece of bread.
I don't know, but a small piece of bread.
And on this bread, they were living.
The Judenrat.
They had power of death.
When the Germans tell them that we need, today, 50 people to go to work, they knew right away that is for dead, but they have to give it to them.
They gave it to them.
They have to give it to them, the 50 people.
Yeah.
They said for work, but they took them, and they were killing them.
Later, we knew about it.
The Jewish population?
They hate them.
They hate them because they took away their families.
Families, they took away.
You take away the head of the family, so the whole family is going down.
And the Judenrat didn't have nothing to do with that.
They told them you have to give me 50, 100 people.
See, my friends, Daniel [INAUDIBLE] and [INAUDIBLE], we studied together in gymnasium.
This was only 3% Jewish.
They were the head of the Judenrat because I was studying French.
They were studying German.
In this ghetto, I was-- this was in December 1941 till April '42.
April 15, '42.
We had doctors.
We had a doctor.
Yeah.
But we didn't have nothing for the sick, to give.
We didn't have nothing.
We didn't have medicine.
They were dying, and that's it.
Listen, better die than to be killed like that.
No.
You see, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this was a war, a terrible war.
A war of survival.
If you could survive yourself, you didn't give a hoot for nobody else.
Mothers and fathers was killing the children because they were in some place in the [INAUDIBLE], and the child was hungry.
And they were crying.
So they killed them by themselves in order not to-- they should not find out that people are here.
